sections.1. Introduction:
Treatment of established AKI is largely supportive. Severe AKI requires RRT. RRT timing relies on occurrence of AKI-related complications. Different modalities have specific indications.

RRT is not without complications and may have important impact on drug pharmacology. Complications are related to: vascular access, anticoagulation, the extracorporeal circuit, treatment settings, and unwanted extracorporeal losses.
sections.2.1 Vascular access:
Major complications include:
• Mechanical complications from insertion: vascular injury, bleeding, pneumothorax
• Catheter-related bloodstream infections (CRBSI)

Catheter insertion should be ultrasound-guided. Catheters no longer needed should be removed as soon as possible.

The right internal jugular vein is generally preferred for CRRT due to optimal flow characteristics and lower dysfunction rates.

Catheter dysfunction remains a major contributor to inadequate RRT delivery and reduced effective dose.
sections.2.2 Anticoagulation:
RCA is recommended as first-line anticoagulation for CRRT in most critically ill patients. Compared with systemic heparin, RCA prolongs filter lifespan and reduces bleeding complications without affecting mortality.

RCA-specific risks: citrate overload and accumulation (discussed in electrolyte/acid-base section).

Safe use of RCA requires protocolised delivery and close monitoring of ionised calcium, total-to-ionised calcium ratio, and acid-base status.

Insufficient anticoagulation → circuit clotting (fibrin deposition, platelet adhesion, complement activation, neutrophil recruitment, cytokine release) and clogging → reduced solute clearance and blood spoliation when circuit is lost. Risk maximal without any anticoagulation.
sections.2.3.1 Hypothermia:
Decrease of body temperature occurs in up to 50% of patients undergoing CRRT.

Consequences: increased energy requirements, shivering, coagulation disorders, masking of fever (delaying infection recognition).

Heating devices vary in effectiveness by machine; may require wrapping venous/arterial lines or additional warming blankets.

Excessive heat transfer from circuit to patient → tachycardia, decreased vascular tone, increased cardiac output, hemodynamic instability. If hyperthermia (>37.5°C) during CRRT: first think of infection, then verify temperature control settings.

Cooling the dialysate is one measure that may improve hemodynamic stability during intermittent HD (Schortgen 2000). Its efficiency for CRRT-related hemodynamic instability is limitedly evaluated.
sections.2.3.2 Membrane bioincompatibility:
No extracorporeal circuit is completely biocompatible. Current semi-synthetic and synthetic membranes (polysulfone, PMMA) have improved biocompatibility compared to older cuprophane-based membranes.

Improved biocompatibility → decreased circulating pro-inflammatory cytokines, maintained circuit patency through inhibition of inflammation-related procoagulant cascade.

Membrane bio-incompatibility may cause:
• Systemic pro-inflammatory response
• Vasodilatory shock with or without clinical signs of anaphylaxis

Management: when other causes of hemodynamic instability are excluded, consider using a filter composed of a different synthetic material.
sections.2.4.1 HIRRT:
Hemodynamic instability related to RRT (HIRRT) is frequent and clinically relevant in both intermittent and continuous RRT. It is a multifactorial phenomenon, not solely caused by hypovolemia. Associated with increased mortality and increasing fluid balance.

Mechanisms:
Decreased cardiac output:
• Decreased preload: mismatch between net ultrafiltration (UFNET) and vascular refilling, deleterious osmotic shifts (Na, urea)
• Tachyarrhythmias from electrolyte disturbances (K, Ca)
• Myocardial stunning

Decreased systemic vascular resistance:
• Vasopressor extracorporeal clearance
• Deleterious heat transfer
• Hypocalcaemia (monitor closely with RCA)
• Membrane bio-incompatibility

UFNET and mortality: U-shaped relationship. Low (<1.0 ml/kg/h) and high (>1.75 ml/kg/h) UFNET both associated with increased mortality. Optimal range: 1.0–1.75 ml/kg/h.

Preload-dependence accounts for only ~50% of HIRRT episodes during CRRT (Chazot 2021).

GO NEUTRAL trial (Bitker 2024): moderate UFNET (100 ml/h, ~1.4 ml/kg/h) guided by functional hemodynamic monitoring improved day-3 fluid balance without increasing hemodynamic instability in vasopressor-dependent patients.

Prevention:
• Prefer CRRT over intermittent RRT in acute circulatory failure; standard blood flow <250 ml/h, effluent 20–25 ml/kg/h
• Delay UFNET if overt hypovolemia
• Apply moderate UFNET once hemodynamic stability achieved, with systematic reassessment
• Prevent electrolyte disturbances; maintain homeostasis (especially with RCA)
• Target neutral or mild hypothermia in acute circulatory failure

Intermittent RRT specific: low blood/dialysis flow, UFNET <500 ml/h, mild hypothermia, high sodium conductivity. Consider daily IHD (vs alternate-day) to optimise fluid removal while reducing HIRRT risk.
sections.2.4.2 Dialysis disequilibrium:
Results from rapid correction of high plasma urea or sodium levels → fluid shifts to intracellular compartment → brain oedema.

Symptoms: headache, dizziness, nausea, restlessness, muscle spasms, disorientation, tremors, seizures, coma, cardiac arrest.

Risk factors: pre-existing brain oedema conditions (stroke, malignant hypertension, hyponatraemia, head trauma, hypoglycaemia). More prone to occur with intermittent RRT (higher solute exchange kinetics).

Prevention:
• Use CRRT in at-risk patients (reduces rate of solute removal)
• Reduce duration of first IHD sessions + reduce blood/dialysate flow
• Low-dose RRT and frequent urea monitoring in CKD patients with high BUN at admission
• Acute brain injury patients: prefer CRRT (especially CVVH) or low-dose IHD to reduce osmotic shifts
sections.2.4.3 Electrolyte and acid-base errors:
After correction of hyperkalaemia and hyperphosphataemia from AKI, many CRRT patients develop:
• Hypokalaemia
• Hypophosphataemia — risk increases with increasing RRT dose. Severe (<0.35 mmol/L): respiratory alkalosis, muscle weakness, prolonged MV
• Hypomagnesaemia — especially with RCA → adverse tachyarrhythmias

Prevention: frequent monitoring (≥daily), adapt K and PO4 content of replacement fluid/dialysate. Many older solutions lack phosphate → extra supplementation needed. Some patients need Mg supplements (especially with RCA).

Sodium: serious Na disorders require adapted replacement fluid/dialysate composition. Modern machines allow Na conductivity settings (130–160 mEq/L). In IHD: balance hemodynamic benefit of Na transfer (increased plasma osmolarity, blood volume support) against sodium loading on fluid balance and fluid shifts. During CRRT: solution Na (~140 mEq/L) not usually manipulated.

Citrate-related disturbances (from Part 4 + updated data): hypocalcaemia, metabolic alkalosis, hypernatraemia, citrate accumulation (4–17% of cases per Müller 2025, Bidar 2025). Accumulation identified by: metabolic acidosis, low ionised Ca, tCa/iCa >2.5, increasing Ca reinjection needs. More associated with impaired cellular metabolism (severe shock) than liver dysfunction alone.
sections.2.4.4 Thrombocytopenia:
Multiple reasons for thrombocytopenia in critically ill AKI patients. Contribution of the extracorporeal circuit is difficult to assess.

In patients receiving heparin: suspect HIT if thrombocytopenia associated with filter clotting and intermediate/high 4Ts score.

Differential diagnoses: sepsis, DIC, medication side-effects.
sections.2.4.5 Loss of nutrients:
Macro- and micronutrients removed by RRT.

Macronutrients: glucose and amino acids (small molecules, readily removed). Balance depends on input: glucose in dialysate/replacement solution, citrate providing energy via Krebs cycle.

Caution: glucose-free replacement solutions → risk of euglycaemic ketoacidosis (especially in severe shock/catabolic stress).

Amino acid losses: 10–15 g or ~15% of infused amino acids with CRRT.

Nutrition guidelines (ESPEN, Fiaccadori 2021):
• Trophic enteral nutrition within 3 days of hospital admission
• Full nutritional support (80–100% of measured caloric expenditure) within 3–7 days
• Protein intake: 1.3–1.5 g/kg/day during IHD, 1.5–1.7 g/kg/day during CRRT

Prolonged RRT: water-soluble trace elements (selenium, zinc, copper) and vitamins (thiamine, folate, vitamin C) may probably require supplementation, although clinical outcome impact uncertain.
sections.3.1 PK Introduction:
Adequate drug dosing (especially antibiotics) is crucial: underdosing → therapeutic failure, resistance; overdosing → toxic effects. Both increase morbidity/mortality.

Literature is heterogeneous due to wide variation in RRT modalities/settings and patient population.

Direct drug-filter interaction may occur → early underdosing (sequestration), late toxicity (spontaneous release).

Providing exact dosing guidelines for individual drugs/patients is illusory, but understanding general principles reduces risk of improper dosing.
sections.3.2 General principles:
Drug dosing during RRT should be individualised, considering:

1. Drugs with easily measurable therapeutic effect (sedatives, haemodynamics drugs) → titrate to clinical effect.

2. Antimicrobial PD targets: fT>MIC (time-dependent: beta-lactams), Cmax/MIC (concentration-dependent: aminoglycosides), AUC/MIC (concentration-dependent with time-dependence).

3. Account for altered PK in critical illness: changed protein binding, hypoalbuminaemia, increased Vd, altered clearance. High inter- and intra-patient variability. Use TDM whenever possible.

4. Early septic shock + RRT: antimicrobial dosing of renally cleared antibiotics should follow non-RRT rules over first 48 hours (increased loading, full maintenance doses), then adapt to RRT intensity, duration, urine output, and/or TDM.

5. CRRT dosing is easier than intermittent (continuous clearance vs two-phase PK).

6. Loading dose depends ONLY on Vd. For hydrophilic drugs (aminoglycosides, beta-lactams), Vd often increased (capillary leak, fluid resuscitation, hypoalbuminaemia) → higher loading dose needed. AKI/RRT does NOT require loading dose alteration.

7. Maintenance dose depends on clearance. Time-dependent: adapt dose/administration mode. Concentration-dependent: adapt dosing interval.

8. Extracorporeal removal clinically important only if >25% of total body clearance → hepatically cleared drugs usually not significantly affected.

9. Limited extracorporeal removal: high Vd (limits plasma concentration) or high protein binding (limits free fraction). Examples: flucloxacillin, ceftriaxone, teicoplanin, clindamycin, cefazolin.

10. Drugs with predominant renal clearance → require dosage adaptation for AKI and RRT.

11. Most important factor for extracorporeal removal = RRT intensity (≈ effluent rate in CRRT, ~30–40 ml/min as CrCl surrogate). Does NOT apply to drugs with important tubular reabsorption (fluconazole, colistin: extracorporeal removal can equal/exceed normal kidney clearance). Opposite for drugs with tubular secretion.

12. Urine output (especially recovery phase) is important for antimicrobial dosing adjustments.

13. Convective transport > diffusive for large MW drugs (vancomycin, teicoplanin, colistin). Combined convective + diffusive > either alone at equal RRT doses.

14. Drug-filter sequestration: hydrophobic synthetic membranes (sulfonated PAN) have high adsorptive capacity; cellulose triacetate at the other end. Important for polymyxin, aminoglycosides; debated for echinocandins.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a recognised mechanism contributing to hemodynamic instability related to RRT (HIRRT)?
options: C: Increased systemic vascular resistance from membrane bioincompatibility
D: Mismatch between net ultrafiltration and vascular refilling
questions.explanation: Membrane bioincompatibility causes DECREASED (not increased) SVR through a vasodilatory pro-inflammatory response. All other options are recognised HIRRT mechanisms: myocardial stunning, vasopressor clearance, preload-refilling mismatch, and electrolyte-triggered arrhythmias.

questions.question: Observational data suggest a U-shaped relationship between net ultrafiltration rate (UFNET) and mortality in ICU patients receiving RRT. Which UFNET range is associated with the lowest risk of death?
questions.explanation: Both low (<1.0 ml/kg/h) and high (>1.75 ml/kg/h) UFNET rates are associated with increased mortality. The optimal intermediate range is 1.0–1.75 ml/kg/h, suggesting that both inadequate fluid removal and overly aggressive ultrafiltration are harmful.

questions.question: During continuous RRT, what proportion of hemodynamic instability episodes is accounted for by preload-dependence (hypovolemia)?
questions.explanation: Chazot et al. (2021) showed that hypovolemia (identified by preload-dependence) accounts for only about half of HIRRT episodes during CRRT. The other half involves non-volume-related mechanisms such as vasodilation, myocardial stunning, and arrhythmias.

questions.question: What is the primary pathophysiological mechanism underlying dialysis disequilibrium syndrome?
options: B: Rapid correction of plasma urea or sodium causing osmotic fluid shifts to the intracellular compartment
C: Citrate accumulation with metabolic acidosis
D: Air embolism from circuit disconnection
questions.explanation: Dialysis disequilibrium results from rapid correction of high plasma urea or sodium, leading to osmotic fluid shifts into the intracellular compartment and brain oedema. Symptoms range from headache and nausea to seizures, coma, and cardiac arrest.

questions.question: A patient with chronic kidney disease and a blood urea of 60 mmol/L is admitted to the ICU. Which RRT strategy best prevents dialysis disequilibrium syndrome?
options: A: High-dose intermittent haemodialysis with maximum blood flow
B: Standard intermittent haemodialysis with sodium profiling
C: CRRT or low-dose IHD with frequent urea monitoring
D: No RRT until urea normalises spontaneously
questions.explanation: In at-risk patients (CKD with high BUN), prevention of dialysis disequilibrium involves reducing the rate of solute removal by using CRRT or low-dose IHD with frequent urea monitoring. Reducing duration of first IHD sessions and lowering blood/dialysate flow also help.

questions.question: Severe hypophosphataemia during CRRT is defined as serum phosphate below which threshold, and is associated with which complication?
options: C: < 0.35 mmol/L; muscle weakness and respiratory failure
questions.explanation: Severe hypophosphataemia is defined as <0.35 mmol/L and is associated with respiratory alkalosis, muscle weakness, and prolonged invasive mechanical ventilation. The risk increases with increasing RRT dose.

questions.question: What proportion of patients undergoing CRRT may experience a decrease in body temperature?
questions.explanation: Hypothermia occurs in up to 50% of patients undergoing CRRT. It increases energy requirements, may induce shivering and coagulation disorders, masks fever, and may delay recognition of infection.

questions.question: A patient on CRRT develops unexplained vasodilatory shock. Other causes of hemodynamic instability have been excluded. The most appropriate next step regarding the circuit is:
options: A: Increase the blood flow rate
C: Switch to a filter composed of a different synthetic material
D: Increase the effluent flow rate
E: Administer a saline bolus through the circuit
questions.explanation: Membrane bioincompatibility may cause a systemic pro-inflammatory response and vasodilatory shock, with or without anaphylaxis signs. When other causes are excluded, consider using a filter made of a different synthetic material.

questions.question: The loading dose of a drug administered to a critically ill patient on RRT depends primarily on which pharmacokinetic parameter?
questions.explanation: The loading dose depends ONLY on the volume of distribution (Vd). For hydrophilic drugs (aminoglycosides, beta-lactams), Vd is often increased in critical illness (capillary leak, fluid resuscitation, hypoalbuminaemia), requiring a higher loading dose. The presence of AKI or RRT does NOT require alteration of the loading dose.

questions.question: The maintenance dose of a drug during RRT requires adaptation based primarily on which parameter?
questions.explanation: The maintenance dose depends on clearance and requires adaptation for reduced clearance in AKI and for extracorporeal-related clearance in RRT. For time-dependent antibiotics, adaptation is through dose/administration mode changes; for concentration-dependent antibiotics, through dosing interval changes.

questions.question: Extracorporeal drug removal during RRT is considered clinically important when it contributes more than what percentage of total body clearance?
questions.explanation: Extracorporeal drug removal is only clinically important if it contributes to more than 25% of total body clearance. This is why drugs with predominant hepatic clearance are usually not significantly affected by RRT.

questions.question: Effluent rate during CRRT can be used as a rough surrogate for creatinine clearance. For which drug does this approximation NOT apply because extracorporeal removal can equal or exceed normal kidney clearance?
questions.explanation: Using effluent rate as a CrCl surrogate (~30–40 ml/min) does not apply to drugs with important tubular reabsorption, such as fluconazole and colistin, where extracorporeal removal can equal or even exceed normal kidney clearance. The opposite is true for drugs with important tubular secretion.

questions.question: For beta-lactam antibiotics, which pharmacodynamic (PD) target should be maximised to achieve therapeutic success?
options: C: fT>MIC (fraction of time above MIC)
questions.explanation: Beta-lactams are time-dependent antibiotics with fT>MIC as the key PD target. Aminoglycosides are concentration-dependent (Cmax/MIC), while some antibiotics like vancomycin use AUC/MIC.

questions.question: Which of the following drugs has LIMITED extracorporeal removal due to high protein binding?
questions.explanation: Drugs with high protein binding (limiting free fraction for removal) and/or high Vd have limited extracorporeal removal. Examples include ceftriaxone, flucloxacillin, teicoplanin, clindamycin, and cefazolin.

questions.question: According to ESPEN guidelines, what is the recommended protein intake for critically ill patients receiving CRRT?
questions.explanation: ESPEN guidelines recommend 1.5–1.7 g/kg/day during CRRT (and 1.3–1.5 g/kg/day during IHD) to compensate for the catabolic protein expenditure from amino acid losses (10–15 g or ~15% of infused amino acids are removed by CRRT).

questions.question: Which type of dialysis membrane has the highest adsorptive capacity for drug sequestration?
questions.explanation: Hydrophobic synthetic membranes such as sulfonated polyacrylonitrile (PAN) have the highest adsorptive capacity for drug sequestration. Cellulose triacetate is at the other end of the spectrum, with unsulfonated PAN and polysulfone in between.

questions.question: During the first 48 hours of septic shock in a patient on RRT, how should renally cleared antimicrobials be dosed?
options: A: Standard renal-adjusted doses from the start
B: Half the normal dose to prevent toxicity
C: Same rules as non-RRT patients: increased loading and full maintenance doses
D: No antimicrobials until RRT is established for 48 hours
E: Dose based solely on effluent flow rate
questions.explanation: In early septic shock with RRT, renally cleared antibiotic dosing should follow non-RRT patient rules to avoid therapeutic failure: increased loading and full maintenance doses for the first 48 hours, followed by adaptation to RRT intensity, duration, urine output and/or TDM.

questions.question: Regarding mechanisms of hemodynamic instability related to RRT (HIRRT), assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Myocardial stunning is a recognised mechanism contributing to HIRRT

statements.label: B
statements.text: Vasopressor extracorporeal clearance can contribute to decreased systemic vascular resistance

statements.label: C
statements.text: HIRRT is solely caused by hypovolemia from ultrafiltration

statements.label: D
statements.text: Electrolyte disturbances (potassium, calcium) can cause tachyarrhythmias contributing to HIRRT

questions.explanation: HIRRT is multifactorial, not solely from hypovolemia. Preload-dependence accounts for only ~50% of episodes. Other mechanisms include myocardial stunning, vasopressor clearance, heat transfer, hypocalcaemia, and membrane bioincompatibility.

questions.question: Regarding dialysis disequilibrium syndrome, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: It results from rapid correction of high plasma urea or sodium levels

statements.label: B
statements.text: Symptoms are caused by brain oedema due to osmotic fluid shifts

statements.label: C
statements.text: It is more prone to occur with CRRT than with intermittent RRT

statements.label: D
statements.text: Patients with acute brain injury should preferably receive CRRT to reduce osmotic shifts

questions.explanation: Dialysis disequilibrium is MORE prone to occur with intermittent RRT due to higher solute exchange kinetics. CRRT is preferred in at-risk patients (including acute brain injury) because it removes solutes more gradually.

questions.question: Regarding electrolyte complications during CRRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Hypophosphataemia risk increases with increasing RRT dose

statements.label: B
statements.text: Many older replacement fluids contain adequate phosphate concentrations

statements.label: C
statements.text: Hypomagnesaemia may occur especially with regional citrate anticoagulation

statements.label: D
statements.text: During CRRT, solution sodium concentration (~140 mEq/L) is routinely manipulated

questions.explanation: HypoPO4 risk increases with RRT dose. Many OLDER replacement fluids do NOT contain phosphate → extra supplementation needed. HypoMg occurs especially with RCA. During CRRT, Na concentration is NOT usually manipulated (unlike IHD where Na conductivity settings are used).

questions.question: Regarding temperature-related complications during CRRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Hypothermia occurs in up to 50% of patients undergoing CRRT

statements.label: B
statements.text: Hypothermia may mask fever and delay recognition of infection

statements.label: C
statements.text: Cooling the dialysate may improve hemodynamic stability during intermittent haemodialysis

statements.label: D
statements.text: Excessive heat transfer from the extracorporeal circuit cannot cause hemodynamic instability

questions.explanation: Excessive heat transfer CAN cause tachycardia, decreased vascular tone, increased cardiac output, and hemodynamic instability. Cooling dialysate may improve hemodynamic stability in IHD (Schortgen 2000), though CRRT evidence is limited.

questions.question: Regarding nutritional losses during RRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: CRRT removes approximately 10–15 g or ~15% of infused amino acids

statements.label: B
statements.text: Recommended protein intake during CRRT is 1.5–1.7 g/kg/day

statements.label: C
statements.text: Glucose-free replacement solutions carry a risk of euglycaemic ketoacidosis

statements.label: D
statements.text: Fat-soluble vitamins are the primary vitamins lost during prolonged RRT

questions.explanation: Water-soluble trace elements (selenium, zinc, copper) and vitamins (thiamine, folate, vitamin C) are the primary micronutrients lost during RRT — not fat-soluble vitamins. Glucose-free solutions risk euglycaemic ketoacidosis especially in shock/catabolic states.

questions.question: Regarding loading dose and maintenance dose of drugs during RRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Loading dose depends on volume of distribution (Vd)

statements.label: B
statements.text: The presence of AKI or RRT requires alteration of the loading dose

statements.label: C
statements.text: Maintenance dose depends on clearance

statements.label: D
statements.text: Volume of distribution for hydrophilic drugs is often decreased in critically ill patients

questions.explanation: Loading dose depends only on Vd. AKI/RRT does NOT require loading dose alteration. Maintenance dose depends on clearance. Vd for hydrophilic drugs (aminoglycosides, beta-lactams) is often INCREASED (not decreased) in critical illness due to capillary leak, fluid resuscitation, and hypoalbuminaemia.

questions.question: Regarding determinants of extracorporeal drug removal during RRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Extracorporeal removal is clinically important if it contributes >25% of total body clearance

statements.label: B
statements.text: Drugs with high protein binding have increased free fraction available for extracorporeal removal

statements.label: C
statements.text: Effluent rate is the most important factor affecting extracorporeal drug removal during CRRT

statements.label: D
statements.text: Convective transport is more effective than diffusive transport for larger molecular weight drugs

questions.explanation: High protein binding LIMITS the free fraction available for removal. Effluent rate (reflecting RRT intensity) is the most important factor. Convective transport is more effective for larger MW drugs (vancomycin, teicoplanin, colistin). Combined convective + diffusive > either alone.

questions.question: Regarding pharmacodynamic targets for antibiotics during RRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: For beta-lactams, the key PD target is fT>MIC (time above MIC)

statements.label: B
statements.text: For aminoglycosides, the key PD target is AUC/MIC

statements.label: C
statements.text: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) should be used whenever possible during RRT

statements.label: D
statements.text: Drug dosing during CRRT is more complex than during intermittent RRT

questions.explanation: Beta-lactams: fT>MIC. Aminoglycosides: Cmax/MIC (not AUC/MIC). TDM should be used whenever possible. CRRT dosing is EASIER (not more complex) than intermittent RRT because of continuous clearance vs two-phase PK (intra- and interdialytic).

questions.question: Regarding vascular access complications during RRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The right internal jugular vein is generally preferred for continuous RRT

statements.label: B
statements.text: Catheter insertion should be ultrasound-guided

statements.label: C
statements.text: Catheter-related bloodstream infections are a major complication of RRT vascular access

statements.label: D
statements.text: Catheter dysfunction has minimal impact on RRT delivery and effective dose

questions.explanation: Right IJV is preferred (optimal flow, lower dysfunction). US-guided insertion is recommended. CRBSI is a major complication. Catheter dysfunction is a MAJOR contributor to inadequate RRT delivery and reduced effective dose — not minimal.

questions.question: Regarding prevention of hemodynamic instability related to RRT (HIRRT), assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: CRRT is preferred over intermittent therapies in patients with acute circulatory failure

statements.label: B
statements.text: Net ultrafiltration should be started immediately regardless of hemodynamic status

statements.label: C
statements.text: The GO NEUTRAL trial showed that moderate UFNET (~1.4 ml/kg/h) guided by hemodynamic monitoring is feasible in vasopressor-dependent patients

statements.label: D
statements.text: Standard effluent flow during CRRT should be 35–40 ml/kg/h to prevent HIRRT

questions.explanation: CRRT preferred in circulatory failure. UFNET should be DELAYED in overt hypovolemia, not started immediately. GO NEUTRAL showed moderate UFNET is feasible with hemodynamic monitoring. Standard effluent flow is 20–25 ml/kg/h (not 35–40).
